title: Kashmiri language

infobox.value: [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") ([Kashmir Valley](/wiki/Kashmir_Valley "Kashmir Valley") and surrounding areas of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"),[[1]](#cite_note-e26-1) parts of northern [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir"))

Official: [Perso-Arabic script](/wiki/Perso-Arabic_script "Perso-Arabic script") (contemporary)[[4]](#cite_note-Mouton_de_Gruyter-4) Others: [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari")[[4]](#cite_note-Mouton_de_Gruyter-4) (informally used by some sections within the [Kashmiri Pandit](/wiki/Kashmiri_Pandit "Kashmiri Pandit") community after 1990),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Sharada script](/wiki/Sharada_script "Sharada script") (ancient/liturgical)[[4]](#cite_note-Mouton_de_Gruyter-4)

infobox.key: This article contains [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") phonetic symbols.
infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:IPA#Rendering_issues "Help:IPA"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") characters. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(Indic) "Help:Multilingual support (Indic)"), you may see boxes or letters that did not properly join into syllables instead of Indic text.

(English: [/kæʃˈmɪəri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*kash-MEER-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[13]](#cite_note-13) also known by its [endonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") **Koshur**[[14]](#cite_note-14) (Kashmiri: کٲشُر ([Perso-Arabic](/wiki/Perso-Arabic "Perso-Arabic"), [Official Script](/wiki/Official_scripts_of_the_Republic_of_India "Official scripts of the Republic of India")), pronounced [[kəːʃur]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")),[[1]](#cite_note-e26-1) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") of the [Dardic](/wiki/Dardic_languages "Dardic languages") branch spoken by around 7 million [Kashmiris](/wiki/Kashmiris "Kashmiris") of the [Kashmir region](/wiki/Kashmir_region "Kashmir region"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) primarily in the [Kashmir Valley](/wiki/Kashmir_Valley "Kashmir Valley") and surrounding hills of the Indian-administrated [union territory](/wiki/Union_territory "Union territory") of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"), over half the population of that territory.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Kashmiri has [split ergativity](/wiki/Split_ergativity "Split ergativity") and the unusual [verb-second word order](/wiki/V2_word_order "V2 word order").

**Kashmiri** (English: [/kæʃˈmɪəri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*kash-MEER-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[13]](#cite_note-13) also known by its [endonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") **Koshur**[[14]](#cite_note-14) (Kashmiri: کٲشُر ([Perso-Arabic](/wiki/Perso-Arabic "Perso-Arabic"), [Official Script](/wiki/Official_scripts_of_the_Republic_of_India "Official scripts of the Republic of India")), pronounced [[kəːʃur]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")),[[1]](#cite_note-e26-1) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") of the [Dardic](/wiki/Dardic_languages "Dardic languages") branch spoken by around 7 million [Kashmiris](/wiki/Kashmiris "Kashmiris") of the [Kashmir region](/wiki/Kashmir_region "Kashmir region"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) primarily in the [Kashmir Valley](/wiki/Kashmir_Valley "Kashmir Valley") and surrounding hills of the Indian-administrated [union territory](/wiki/Union_territory "Union territory") of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"), over half the population of that territory.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Kashmiri has [split ergativity](/wiki/Split_ergativity "Split ergativity") and the unusual [verb-second word order](/wiki/V2_word_order "V2 word order").

Since 2020, it has been made an official language of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)") along with [Dogri](/wiki/Dogri_language "Dogri language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and English.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Kashmiri is also among the 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") of India.

Kashmiri is spoken by roughly five percent of Pakistani-administrated [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir")'s population.[[18]](#cite_note-Bukhari-18)

## Geographic distribution and status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geographic distribution and status")]

There are about 6.8 million speakers of Kashmiri and related dialects in Jammu and Kashmir and amongst the [Kashmiri diaspora](/wiki/Kashmiri_diaspora "Kashmiri diaspora") in other states of India.[[19]](#cite_note-Census2011-19) Most Kashmiri speakers are located in the [Kashmir Valley](/wiki/Kashmir_Valley "Kashmir Valley") and other surrounding areas of Jammu and Kashmir.[[20]](#cite_note-Koshur-20) In the Kashmir Valley, Kashmiri speakers form the majority.

The Kashmiri language is one of the [22 scheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") of [India](/wiki/India "India").[[21]](#cite_note-Central_Institute_of_Indian_Languages-21) It was a part of the *Eighth Schedule* in the former constitution of Jammu and Kashmir. Along with other regional languages mentioned in the *Sixth Schedule*, as well as Hindi and Urdu, the Kashmiri language was to be developed in the state.[[22]](#cite_note-GAD-22) After [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), Kashmiri is the second fastest growing [language of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"), followed by [Meitei](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language") ([Manipuri](/wiki/Manipuri_language "Manipuri language")) as well as [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language") in the third place, and [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") in the fourth place, according to the [2011 census of India](/wiki/2011_census_of_India "2011 census of India").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Persian began to be used as the court language in Kashmir during the 14th centuries, under the influence of Islam. It was replaced by Urdu in 1889 during the [Dogra rule](/wiki/Dogra_dynasty "Dogra dynasty").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 2020, Kashmiri became an official language in the Union Territory of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(Union_Territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (Union Territory)") for the first time.[[26]](#cite_note-OfficialLang-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Poguli](/wiki/Poguli "Poguli") and [Kishtwari](/wiki/Kishtwari "Kishtwari") are closely related to Kashmiri, which are spoken in the mountains to the south of the Kashmir Valley and have sometimes been counted as dialects of Kashmiri.

Kashmiri is spoken by roughly five percent of [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir")'s population.[[18]](#cite_note-Bukhari-18) According to the [1998 Pakistan Census](/wiki/1998_Pakistan_Census "1998 Pakistan Census"), there were 132,450 Kashmiri speakers in Azad Kashmir.[[29]](#cite_note-Shakil2-29) Native speakers of the language were dispersed in "pockets" throughout Azad Kashmir,[[30]](#cite_note-Kachru2-30)[[31]](#cite_note-KJLR2-31) particularly in the districts of [Muzaffarabad](/wiki/Muzaffarabad_District "Muzaffarabad District") (15%), [Neelam](/wiki/Neelum_District "Neelum District") (20%) and [Hattian](/wiki/Hattian_Bala_District "Hattian Bala District") (15%), with very small minorities in [Haveli](/wiki/Haveli_District "Haveli District") (5%) and [Bagh](/wiki/Bagh_District "Bagh District") (2%).[[29]](#cite_note-Shakil2-29) The Kashmiri spoken in Muzaffarabad is distinct from, although still [intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with, the Kashmiri of the Neelam Valley to the north.[[31]](#cite_note-KJLR2-31) In Neelam Valley, Kashmiri is the second most widely spoken language and the majority language in at least a dozen or so villages, where in about half of these, it is the sole mother tongue.[[31]](#cite_note-KJLR2-31) The Kashmiri dialect of Neelum is closer to the variety spoken in northern Kashmir Valley, particularly [Kupwara](/wiki/Kupwara_district "Kupwara district").[[31]](#cite_note-KJLR2-31) At the [2017 Census of Pakistan](/wiki/2017_Census_of_Pakistan "2017 Census of Pakistan"), as many as 350,000 people declared their first language to be Kashmiri.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

A process of [language shift](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift") is observable among Kashmiri-speakers in Azad Kashmir according to linguist [Tariq Rahman](/wiki/Tariq_Rahman "Tariq Rahman"), as they gradually adopt local languages such as [Pahari-Pothwari](/wiki/Pahari-Pothwari "Pahari-Pothwari"), [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") or move towards the *lingua franca* [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu").[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[30]](#cite_note-Kachru2-30)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[31]](#cite_note-KJLR2-31) This has resulted in these languages gaining ground at the expense of Kashmiri.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) There have been calls for the promotion of Kashmiri at an official level; in 1983, a Kashmiri Language Committee was set up by the government to patronise Kashmiri and impart it in school-level education. However, the limited attempts at introducing the language have not been successful, and it is Urdu, rather than Kashmiri, that Kashmiri Muslims of [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") have seen as their identity symbol.[[38]](#cite_note-Rahman2-38) Rahman notes that efforts to organise a Kashmiri language movement have been challenged by the scattered nature of the Kashmiri-speaking community in Azad Kashmir.[[38]](#cite_note-Rahman2-38)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Kashmiri has a very large phoneme inventory: 32 vowels and 62 consonants, giving that vowel [nasalization](/wiki/Nasalization "Nasalization") and consonant [palatalization](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)") are [phonemic](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") and not [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetics "Phonetics").[[39]](#cite_note-39) It has the following phonemes.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali20069–16-41)

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Vowels")]

The oral vowels are as follows:

:   |  | [Front](/wiki/Front_vowel "Front vowel") | [Central](/wiki/Central_vowel "Central vowel") | [Back](/wiki/Back_vowel "Back vowel") |
    | --- | --- | --- | --- |
    | [High](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") | [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") [iː](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") | [ɨ](/wiki/Close_central_unrounded_vowel "Close central unrounded vowel") [ɨː](/wiki/Close_central_unrounded_vowel "Close central unrounded vowel") | [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") [uː](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") |
    | [Mid](/wiki/Mid_vowel "Mid vowel") | [e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") [eː](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") | [ə](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel") [əː](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel") | [o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") [oː](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") |
    | [Low](/wiki/Open_vowel "Open vowel") |  | [a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") [aː](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") | [ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel") [ɔː](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel") |

The short high vowels are [near-high](/wiki/Near-high_vowel "Near-high vowel"), and the low vowels apart from /aː/ are [near-low](/wiki/Near-low_vowel "Near-low vowel").

[Nasalization](/wiki/Nasalization "Nasalization") is phonemic. All sixteen oral vowels have nasal counterparts.

### Archaisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Archaisms")]

Kashmiri, as also the other Dardic languages, shows important divergences from the Indo-Aryan mainstream. One is the partial maintenance of the three [sibilant consonants](/wiki/Sibilant "Sibilant") *s ṣ ś* of the Old Indo-Aryan period. For another example, the prefixing form of the number 'two', which is found in Sanskrit as *dvi-*, has developed into *ba-/bi-* in most other Indo-Aryan languages, but *du-* in Kashmiri (preserving the original dental stop *d*). *Seventy-two* is *dusatath* in Kashmiri, *bahattar* in Hindi-Urdu and Punjabi, and *dvisaptati* in Sanskrit.[[42]](#cite_note-ref41yomid-42)

There are three [orthographical systems](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") used to write the Kashmiri language: the [Perso-Arabic script](/wiki/Perso-Arabic_script "Perso-Arabic script"), the [Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari_script "Devanagari script") and the [Sharada script](/wiki/Sharada_script "Sharada script"). The [Roman script](/wiki/Roman_script "Roman script") is also sometimes informally used to write Kashmiri, especially online.[[4]](#cite_note-Mouton_de_Gruyter-4)

The Kashmiri language was traditionally written in the [Sharada script](/wiki/Sharada_script "Sharada script") from the 8th Century AD onwards.[[43]](#cite_note-Sharada-43) Between the 8th and the first quarter of the 20th century AD, Sharada was the primary script of inscriptional and literary production in Kashmir for [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and Kashmiri.[[44]](#cite_note-sharada-44) With increased use of Persian script for writing Kashmiri in the 19th century AD, and the growth of other [brahmic scripts](/wiki/Brahmic_script "Brahmic script") such as Devanagari and [Takri](/wiki/Takri "Takri"), the use of Sharada declined.[[44]](#cite_note-sharada-44) The Sharada script is inadequate for writing modern Kashmiri because it lacks sufficient signs to represent Kashmiri vowels.[[44]](#cite_note-sharada-44) Modern usage of Sharada is limited to religious ceremonies and rituals of [Kashmiri Pandits](/wiki/Kashmiri_Pandit "Kashmiri Pandit"), and for [horoscope](/wiki/Horoscope "Horoscope")-writing by them.[[45]](#cite_note-KOA-Sharada-45)[[44]](#cite_note-sharada-44)

Today Kashmiri is primarily written in Perso-Arabic (with some modifications, such as additions of new signs to represent Kashmiri vowels).[[46]](#cite_note-Omniglot-Kashmiri-46)[[44]](#cite_note-sharada-44) Among languages written in the Perso-Arabic script, Kashmiri is one of the scripts that regularly indicates all vowel sounds.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The Kashmiri Perso-Arabic script is recognized as the official script of Kashmiri language by the Jammu and Kashmir government and the [Jammu and Kashmir Academy of Art, Culture and Languages](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_Academy_of_Art,_Culture_and_Languages "Jammu and Kashmir Academy of Art, Culture and Languages").[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Kashmiri Perso-Arabic script has been derived from [Persian alphabet](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet"). The consonant inventory and their corresponding pronunciations of Kashmiri Perso-Arabic script doesn't differ from Perso-Arabic script, with the exception of the letter [ژ](/wiki/%C5%BDe "Že"), which is pronounced as /[t͡s](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_affricate "Voiceless alveolar affricate")/ instead of /[ʒ](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_fricative "Voiced postalveolar fricative")/. However, the vowel inventory of Kashmiri is significantly larger than other Perso-Arabic derived or influenced South Asian Perso-Arabic scripts. There are 17 vowels in Kashmiri, shown with [diacritics](/wiki/Arabic_diacritics "Arabic diacritics"), letters ([alif](/wiki/Aleph "Aleph"), [waw](/wiki/Waw_(letter) "Waw (letter)"), [ye](/wiki/Yodh "Yodh")), or both. In Kashmiri, the convention is that most vowel diacritics are written at all times.

Despite Kashmiri Perso-Arabic script cutting across religious boundaries and being used by both the [Kashmiri Hindus](/wiki/Kashmiri_Hindus "Kashmiri Hindus") and the [Kashmiri Muslims](/wiki/Kashmiri_Muslims "Kashmiri Muslims"),[[52]](#cite_note-52) some attempts have been made to give a religious outlook regarding the script and make Kashmiri Perso-Arabic script to be associated with [Kashmiri Muslims](/wiki/Kashmiri_Muslims "Kashmiri Muslims"), while the Kashmiri Devanagari script to be associated with some sections of [Kashmiri Hindu](/wiki/Kashmiri_Hindus "Kashmiri Hindus") community.[[53]](#cite_note-Indian_Express-Perso-Arabic-53)[[54]](#cite_note-KOA-Devanagari-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

There have been a few versions of the Devanagari script for Kashmiri.[[59]](#cite_note-66)
The 2002 version of the proposal is shown below.[[60]](#cite_note-67) This version has readers and more content available on the Internet, even though this is an older proposal.[[61]](#cite_note-68)[[62]](#cite_note-69)
This version makes use of the vowels ॲ/ऑ and vowel signs
कॅ/कॉ for the schwa-like vowel [ə] and elongated schwa-like vowel [əː] that also exist in other Devanagari-based scripts such as Marathi and Hindi but are used for the sound of other vowels.

Tabulated below is the latest (2009) version of the proposal to spell the Kashmiri vowels with Devanagari.[[63]](#cite_note-70)[[64]](#cite_note-71)
The primary change in this version is the changed stand alone characters ॳ / ॴ and vowel signs कऺ / कऻ for the schwa-like vowel [ə] & elongated schwa-like vowel [əː] and a new stand alone vowel ॵ and vowel sign कॏ for the open-mid back rounded vowel [ɔ] which can be used instead of the consonant व standing-in for this vowel.

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Kashmiri is a [fusional language](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language")[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200625-75) with [verb-second (V2)](/wiki/V2_word_order "V2 word order") word order.[[69]](#cite_note-76) Several of Kashmiri's grammatical features distinguish it from other [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali2006ii-77)

### Nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Nouns")]

Kashmiri nouns are inflected according to gender, number and case. There are no [articles](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)"), nor is there any grammatical distinction for [definiteness](/wiki/Definiteness "Definiteness"), although there is some optional adverbial marking for indefinite or "generic" noun qualities.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200625-75)

#### Gender

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Gender")]

The Kashmiri [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") system is divided into masculine and feminine. Feminine forms are typically generated by the addition of a suffix (or in most cases, a [morphophonemic](/wiki/Morphophonology "Morphophonology") change, or both) to a masculine noun.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200625-75) A relatively small group of feminine nouns have unique [suppletion](/wiki/Suppletion "Suppletion") forms that are totally different from the corresponding masculine forms.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200628-78) The following table illustrates the range of possible gender forms:[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200626–28-79)

:   :   | Process | Masculine | Feminine | Meaning |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | -en’ suffix | [d̪ukaːnd̪aːr] دُکاندار | [d̪ukaːnd̪aːrenʲ] دُکانداریٚنؠ | shopkeeper |
        | -bāy suffix | [maːʃʈar] ماشٹَر | [maːʃʈarbaːj] ماشٹَر باے | teacher |
        | -in’ + vowel change | [xar] خَر | [xərinʲ] خٔرِنؠ | donkey |
        | -ür + vowel change | [pʰot̪] پھۆت | [pʰɔt̪ɨr] پھۄتٕر | basket |
        | Adding of affix | [huːn] ہوٗن | [huːnʲ] ہوٗنؠ | dog/bitch |
        | vowel change | [ɡaɡur] گَگُر | [ɡaɡɨr] گَگٕر | rat |
        | consonant change | [hokʰ] ہۆکھ | [hot͡ʃʰ] ہۆچھ | dry |
        | vowel/consonant change | [t̪ot̪] تۆت | [t̪ət͡s] تٔژ | hot |
        | suppletive form | [marɨd̪] مَرٕد | [zanaːnɨ] زَنانہٕ | man/woman |
        | masculine only | [kʲom] کیٛۆم | --- | insect |
        | feminine only | --- | [mət͡ʃʰ] مٔچھ | housefly |

Some nouns borrowed from other languages, such as Persian, Arabic, Sanskrit, Urdu or English, follow a slightly different gender system. Notably, many words borrowed from Urdu have different genders in Kashmiri.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200628-78)

#### Case

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Case")]

There are five [cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") in Kashmiri: [nominative](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case"), [dative](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case"), [ergative](/wiki/Ergative_case "Ergative case"), [ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case") and [vocative](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case").[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200631-80) Case is expressed via suffixation of the noun.

Kashmiri utilizes an [ergative-absolutive case structure](/wiki/Ergative-absolutive_language "Ergative-absolutive language") when the verb is in simple past tense.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200631-80) Thus, in these sentences, the [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") of a [transitive verb](/wiki/Transitive_verb "Transitive verb") is marked in the ergative case and the [object](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)") in nominative, which is identical to how the subject of an [intransitive verb](/wiki/Intransitive_verb "Intransitive verb") is marked.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200631-80)[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWade188816-81)[[75]](#cite_note-82) However, in sentences constructed in any other tense, or in past tense sentences with intransitive verbs, a nominative-dative paradigm is adopted, with objects (whether direct or indirect) generally marked in dative case.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200632-83)
Other case distinctions, such as [locative](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case"), [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case"), [comitative](/wiki/Comitative_case "Comitative case") and [allative](/wiki/Allative_case "Allative case"), are marked by [postpositions](/wiki/Preposition_and_postposition "Preposition and postposition") rather than suffixation.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200639-84)

#### Noun morphology

The following table illustrates Kashmiri noun declension according to gender, number and case.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200632-83)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWade188810–15-85)

:   :   |  | Masculine | | Feminine | |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | singular | plural | singular | plural |
        | Nom. | -Ø | -Ø | -Ø | -Ø |
        | Erg. | -[an] اَن | -[aʋ] اَو | -[i] اِ | -[aʋ] اَو |
        | Dat. | -[as] or -[is] اَس or اِس | -[an] اَن | -[i] اِ | -[an] اَن |
        | Abl. | -[i] or -[ɨ] اِ or إ | -[aʋ] اَو | -[i] اِ | -[aʋ] اَو |
        | Voc. | -[aː] ا | -[aʋ] اَو | -[ij] اِے | -[aʋ] اَو |

### Verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Verbs")]

Kashmiri verbs are declined according to tense and [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"), and to a lesser extent, gender. Tense, along with certain distinctions of [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect"), is formed by the addition of suffixes to the verb stem (minus the [infinitive](/wiki/Infinitive "Infinitive") ending - /un/), and in many cases by the addition of various [modal auxiliaries](/wiki/Modal_verb "Modal verb").[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200683–84-86) Postpositions fulfill numerous adverbial and semantic roles.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali2006119-87)

#### Tense

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tense")]

Present tense in Kashmiri is an auxiliary construction formed by a combination of the [copula](/wiki/Copula_(linguistics) "Copula (linguistics)") and the imperfective suffix -/aːn/ added to the verb stem. The various copula forms agree with their subject according to gender and number, and are provided below with the verb /jun/ (to come):[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200684-88)

:   :   Present

        |  | Masculine | Feminine |
        | --- | --- | --- |
        | 1st person sing. | [t͡ʃʰus jiʋaːn] چھُس یِوان | [t͡ʃʰas jiʋaːn] چھَس یِوان |
        | 2nd person sing. | [t͡ʃʰukʰ jiʋaːn] چھُکھ یِوان | [t͡ʃʰakʰ jiʋaːn] چھَکھ یِوان |
        | 3rd person sing. | [t͡ʃʰu jiʋaːn] چھُ یِوان | [t͡ʃʰe jiʋaːn] چھےٚ یِوان |
        | 1st person pl. | [t͡ʃʰi jiʋaːn] چھِ یِوان | [t͡ʃʰa jiʋaːn] چھَ یِوان |
        | 2nd person pl. | [t͡ʃʰiʋ jiʋaːn] چھِو یِوان | [t͡ʃʰaʋ jiʋaːn] چھَو یِوان |
        | 3rd person pl. | [t͡ʃʰi jiʋaːn] چھِ یِوان | [t͡ʃʰe jiʋaːn] چھےٚ یِوان |

Past tense in Kashmiri is significantly more complex than the other tenses, and is subdivided into three past tense distinctions.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200686-89) The simple (sometimes called proximate) past refers to completed past actions. Remote past refers to actions that lack this in-built perfective aspect. Indefinite past refers to actions performed a long time ago, and is often used in historical narrative or storytelling contexts.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200687-90)

As described above, Kashmiri is a [split-ergative](/wiki/Split_ergativity "Split ergativity") language; in all three of these past tense forms, the subjects of transitive verbs are marked in the ergative case and direct objects in the nominative. Intransitive subjects are marked in the nominative.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200687-90) Nominative arguments, whether subjects or objects, dictate gender, number and person marking on the verb.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200687-90)[[84]](#cite_note-91)

Verbs of the simple past tense are formed via the addition of a suffix to the verb stem, which usually undergoes certain uniform morphophonemic changes. First and third person verbs of this type do not take suffixes and agree with the nominative object in gender and number, but there are second person verb endings. The entire simple past tense paradigm of transitive verbs is illustrated below using the verb /parun/ ("to read"):[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200689–90-92)

:   :   Simple past (transitive)

        |  | | Masculine | | Feminine | |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | singular | plural | singular | plural |
        | 1st person | | [por] پۆر | [pərʲ] پٔرؠ | [pər] پٔر | [pari] پَرِ |
        | 2nd person | Non-honorific | [porut̪ʰ] پۆرُتھ | [pərit̪ʰ] پٔرِتھ | [pərɨt̪ʰ] پٔرٕتھ | [parʲat̪ʰ] پَرؠتھ |
        | Honorific | [porʋɨ] پۆروٕ | [pəriʋɨ] پٔرِوٕ | [pərʋɨ] پٔروٕ | [pariʋɨ] پَرِوٕ |
        | 3rd person | | [por] پۆر | [pərʲ] پٔرؠ | [pər] پٔر | [pari] پَرِ |

A group of irregular intransitive verbs (*special intransitives*), take a different set of endings in addition to the morphophonemic changes that affect most past tense verbs.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200691–92-93)

:   :   Simple past (special intransitive)

        |  | Masculine | | Feminine | |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | singular | plural | singular | plural |
        | 1st person | -[us] اُس | -[ʲ] ؠ | -[as] اَس | -[i] اِ |
        | 2nd person | -[kʰ] کھ | -[ʋɨ] وٕ | -[kʰ] کھ | -[ʋɨ] وٕ |
        | 3rd person | -Ø | -Ø | -[t͡ʃʰ] چھ | -[i] اِ |

Intransitive verbs in the simple past are conjugated the same as intransitives in the indefinite past tense form.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200693-94)

:   :   Simple past (intransitive)

        |  | Masculine | | Feminine | |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | singular | plural | singular | plural |

#### Aspect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Aspect")]

There are two main [aspectual](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") distinctions in Kashmiri, perfective and imperfective. Both employ a [participle](/wiki/Participle "Participle") formed by the addition of a suffix to the verb stem, as well as the fully conjugated auxiliary /aːsun/ ("to be")—which agrees according to gender, number and person with the object (for transitive verbs) or the subject (for intransitive verbs).[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali2006105-101)

Like the auxiliary, the participle suffix used with the perfective aspect (expressing completed or concluded action) agrees in gender and number with the object (for transitive verbs) or subject (for intransitives) as illustrated below:[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali2006105-101)

:   :   | Masculine | | Feminine | |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | singular | plural | singular | plural |
        | -[mut̪] مُت | -[mɨt̪ʲ] مٕتؠ | -[mɨt͡s] مٕژ | -[mat͡sɨ] مَژٕ |

The imperfective (expressing habitual or progressive action) is simpler, taking the participle suffix -/aːn/ in all forms, with only the auxiliary showing agreement.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali2006107-102) A type of [iterative aspect](/wiki/Iterative_aspect "Iterative aspect") can be expressed by [reduplicating](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication") the imperfective participle.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali2006108-103)

### Adjectives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Adjectives")]

There are two kinds of adjectives in Kashmiri, those that agree with their referent noun (according to case, gender and number) and those that are not declined at all.[[99]](#cite_note-106) Most adjectives are declined, and generally take the same endings and gender-specific stem changes as nouns.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWade188819-107) The declinable adjective endings are provided in the table below, using the adjective وۄزُل [ʋɔzul] ("red"):[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWade188820-108)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoulWali200659-109)

:   :   |  | Masculine | | Feminine | |
        | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
        | singular | plural | singular | plural |
        | Nom. | [ʋɔzul] وۄزُل | [ʋɔzɨlʲ] وۄزٕلؠ | [ʋɔzɨd͡ʒ] وۄزٕج | [ʋɔzd͡ʒi] وۄزجہِ |
        | Erg. | [ʋɔzlɨ] وۄزلہٕ | [ʋɔzlʲaʋ] وۄزلؠو | [ʋɔzd͡ʒi] وۄزجہِ | [ʋɔzd͡ʒaʋ] وۄزجَو |
        | Dat. | [ʋɔzlis] وۄزلِس | [ʋɔzlʲan] وۄزلؠن | [ʋɔzd͡ʒi] وۄزجہِ | [ʋɔzd͡ʒan] وۄزجَن |
        | Abl. | [ʋɔzlɨ] وۄزلہٕ | [ʋɔzlʲaʋ] وۄزلؠو | [ʋɔzd͡ʒi] وۄزجہِ | [ʋɔzd͡ʒaʋ] وۄزجَو |

Among those adjectives not declined are adjectives that end in -[lad̪] or -[ɨ], adjectives borrowed from other languages, and a few isolated irregulars.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWade188820-108)

The comparative and superlative forms of adjectives are formed with the words ژۆر [t͡sor] ("more") and سؠٹھا [sʲaʈʰaː] ("most"), respectively.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWade188821-110)

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

Kashmiri is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") and was heavily influenced by [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), especially early on.[[106]](#cite_note-113)[[107]](#cite_note-114)[[108]](#cite_note-Gorekar2002-115) After the arrival of [Islamic rule in India](/wiki/Muslim_conquests_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquests in the Indian subcontinent"), Kashmiri acquired many [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") loanwords.[[108]](#cite_note-Gorekar2002-115) In modern times, Kashmiri vocabulary has imported words from
[English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language").[[109]](#cite_note-116)

### Preservation of old Indo-Aryan vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Preservation of old Indo-Aryan vocabulary")]

Kashmiri retains several features of [Old Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Old_Indo-Aryan "Old Indo-Aryan") that have been lost in other modern Indo-Aryan languages such as Hindi-Urdu, Punjabi and Sindhi.[[42]](#cite_note-ref41yomid-42) Some vocabulary features that Kashmiri preserves clearly date from the [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") era and had already been lost even in Classical Sanskrit. This includes the word-form *yodvai* (meaning *if*), which is mainly found in Vedic Sanskrit texts. Classical Sanskrit and modern Indo-Aryan use the word *yadi* instead.[[42]](#cite_note-ref41yomid-42)

### First person pronoun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: First person pronoun")]

Both the Indo-Aryan and Iranian branches of the Indo-Iranian family have demonstrated a strong tendency to eliminate the distinctive first person pronoun ("I") used in the nominative (subject) case. The [Indo-European root](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_pronouns "Proto-Indo-European pronouns") for this is reconstructed as \*eǵHom, which is preserved in Sanskrit as *aham* and in Avestan Persian as *azam*. This contrasts with the *m-* form ("me", "my") that is used for the accusative, genitive, dative, ablative cases. Sanskrit and Avestan both used forms such as *ma(-m)*. However, in languages such as Modern Persian, Baluchi, Hindi and Punjabi, the distinct nominative form has been entirely lost and replaced with *m-* in words such as *ma-n* and *mai*. However, Kashmiri belongs to a relatively small set that preserves the distinction. 'I' is *ba/bi/bo* in various Kashmiri dialects, distinct from the other *me* terms. 'Mine' is *myon* in Kashmiri. Other Indo-Aryan languages that preserve this feature include [Dogri](/wiki/Dogri_language "Dogri language") (*aun* vs *me-*), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language") (*hu-n* vs *ma-ri*), [Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language") (*hā̃v* vs *mhazo*), and [Braj](/wiki/Braj_Bhasa "Braj Bhasa") (*hau-M* vs *mai-M*). The Iranian [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language") preserves it too (*za* vs. *maa*), as well as [Nuristani languages](/wiki/Nuristani_languages "Nuristani languages"), such as [Askunu](/wiki/Askunu_language "Askunu language") (*âi* vs *iũ*).[[110]](#cite_note-ref48bubag-117)

### Variations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Variations")]

There are very minor differences between the Kashmiri spoken by Hindus and Muslims.[[111]](#cite_note-ref16muduc-118) For 'fire', a traditional Hindu uses the word اۆگُن [oɡun] while a Muslim more often uses the Arabic word نار [naːr].[[112]](#cite_note-Kasref-119)

### Perso-Arabic script

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Perso-Arabic script")]

Art. 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights"):

سٲری اِنسان چھِ آزاد زامٕتؠ۔ وؠقار تہٕ حۆقوٗق چھِ ہِوی۔ تِمَن چھُ سوچ سَمٕج عَطا کَرنہٕ آمُت تہٕ تِمَن پَزِ بٲے بَرادٔری ہٕنٛدِس جَذباتَس تَحَت اَکھ أکِس اَکار بَکار یُن ۔[[113]](#cite_note-120)

[səːriː insaːn t͡ʃʰi aːzaːd zaːmɨtʲ. ʋʲaqaːr tɨ hoquːq t͡ʃʰi hiʋiː. timan t͡ʃʰu soːt͡ʃ samɨd͡ʒ ataː karnɨ aːmut tɨ timan pazi bəːj baraːdəriː hɨndis d͡ʒazbaːtas tahat akʰ əkis akaːr bakaːr jun]

"All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood."

[oːmuj akuj at͡ʃʰur porum, suj maːli roʈum ʋɔndas manz, suj maːli kani pʲaʈʰ gorum tɨ t͡sorum, əːsɨs saːs tɨ sapnis sɔn.]

[akuj omkaːr jus naːbi dareː, kumbeː brahmaːnɖas sum gareː, akʰ suj mantʰɨr t͡sʲatas kareː, tas saːs mantʰɨr kʲaːh kareː.]

One who recites the divine word "Omkār" by devotion is capable to build a bridge between his own and the cosmic consciousness. By staying committed to this sacred word, one doesn't require any other mantra out of thousands others.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kashmiri_language&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-58)** At the beginning of a word it can either come with diacritic, or it can be stand-alone and silent, succeeded by a vowel letter. Diacritics  اَ اِ، اُ can be omitted in writing. Other diacritics (i.e. آ، أ، ٲ، إ، اٟ) are never omitted. For example, اَخبار *"akhbār"* is often written as اخبار, whereas أچھ *" ȧchh"* is never written as اچھ.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-59)** Used mainly for [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") loanwords.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-60)** The letter *wāw* can either represent consonant ([ʋ]) or vowel ([oː]). It can also act as a carrier of vowel diacritics, representing several other vowels وٗ, ۆ, ۄ (uː], [o], [ɔ]). At the beginning of a word, when representing a consonant, the letter *wāw* will appear as a standalone character, followed by the appropriate vowel. If representing a vowel at the beginning of a word, the letter *wāw* needs to be preceded by an *ạlif*, او, اوٗ, اۆ, اۄ.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-61)** This letter differs from *do-chashmi hē* (ھ) and they are not interchangeable. Similar to Urdu,*do-chashmi hē* (ھ) is exclusively used as a second part of [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") for representing [aspirated consonants](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-62)** In initial and medial position, the letter *hē* always represents the consonant [h]. In final position, The letter *hē* can either represent consonant ([h]) or vowel ([a]). In final position, only in its attached form, and not in isolated form, it can also act as a carrier of vowel diacritics, representing several other vowels ـٔہ, ـہٕ ([ə], [ɨ]). For example, whereas a final "-rạ" is written as ـرٔ, a final "-gạ" is written as ـگٔہ.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-63)** The letter *yē* can either represent consonant ("y" [j]) or vowel ("ē" [eː] or "ī" [iː]). The letter *yē* can represent [j] in initial or medial position, or it can represent "ē" [eː] or "ī" [iː] in medial positions, or "ī" [iː] in final position. In combination with specific diacritics, the letter *yē* in its medial position, can represent "ī" [iː], "e" [e], "ĕ" [ʲa], or ' [◌ʲ] as well. To represent the consonant "y" [j] or the vowel "ē" [eː] in final position, the letter *boḍ yē* (ے) is used. The letter *boḍ yē* (ے), in combination with specific diacritics, can represent "e" [e] in final position.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-64)** The letter *boḍ yē* only occurs in final position. The letter *boḍ yē* represents the consonant "y" [j] or the vowel "ē" [eː]. With specific diacritics, vowel "e" [e] is also shown with the letter *boḍ yē*.
